data.title: IWMI: Russell 2000 Covered Call ETF, Outstanding Double-Digit Distribution Yield, Strong Performance Track-Record
data.description:
The NEOS Russell 2000 High Income ETF (IWMI) tracks the Russell 2000 small-cap equity index and writes covered calls on a portion of its holdings. IWMI's approach significantly limits upside potential but boosts its distribution yield to 13.6%. A reasonable tradeoff, and one which should be of particular interest to income investors.

In general, IWMI's strategy should outperform when markets are flat or trending downwards, due to its strong distributions. On the flipside, it should underperform during bull markets, due to its limited upside potential. Timing plays an important role, for the fund's equity portfolio and covered call strategy both.

IWMI has performed in-line with broader U.S. equity indexes since inception, reasonably good results considering we've seen significant equity market gains these past few years, of the sort that almost always lead to covered call strategies underperforming. In my opinion, IWMI's strong distribution yield and performance track-record make the fund a buy.

IWMI - Overview and Analysis

Equity Investments

IWMI tracks the Russell 2000 small-cap equity index and writes covered calls on a portion of its holdings.

Russell 2000 exposure is gained through an investment in the Vanguard Russell 2000 Index Fund ETF Shares (VTWO), a simple index ETF tracking said index. IWMI's approach means investors are liable for two management fees, with VTWO charging investors 0.07%, IWMI the much higher figure of 0.61%, or 0.68% in total. IWMI is a relatively pricey ETF, a significant, straightforward negative for investors.

The Russell 2000 index is a simple, broad-based index of small-cap U.S. equities. It differs from the S&P 500 in several important ways.

For starters, the Russell is significantly underweight tech, due to the outsized role that mega-cap tech stocks play in the S&P 500. On the flipside, it is overweight several other industries, including financials, health care, and industrials. Sector drifts are as follows:

Due to the above, the Russell's relative performance is somewhat dependent on the relative performance of the tech industry. Expect the index to outperform when tech underperforms, as was the case during the 2000s:

Expect the index to underperform when tech outperforms, as has been the case these past ten years:

Other factors impact equity returns too, so index performance might not necessarily move with tech performance. The impact should be felt regardless.

Small-cap equities are riskier than average, so these should underperform during most downturns and recessions. As an example, VTWO's losses exceeded those of the S&P 500 by around 10% during the pandemic. Specific figures varied, but spreads remained at around 10% for many months.

As the increased risk here is a bit indirect, and as the Russell differs from the S&P 500 in several ways, it isn't certain that the small-cap index will underperform during the next recession. Seems likelier than not though.

The Russell 2000 is a small-cap equity index, which tend to trade at a discount to large-cap equities due to their greater risk. Valuation metrics are as follows:

In my opinion, the small-cap equity investment thesis rests on their cheap valuations. Benefits here seem pretty straightforward, although there is a lot of uncertainty to these. Valuations could always remain depressed for the foreseeable future, and there hasn't been significant, consistent progress on this front either.

Covered Calls

IWMI tracks the Russell 2000 index and writes covered calls on a portion of its holdings. The fund's covered call strategy is active, so specifics change, although the overall or general impact of these is straightforward.

IWMI's covered calls significantly reduce the fund's upside potential. As an example, IWMI's share price is up 8.7% these past six months, compared to 17.1% in gains for VTWO.

Downside potential remains unchanged, however. As an example, IWMI's share price declined by 13.1% during early 2025, compared to VTWO's 11.8% losses.

As upside potential is reduced while downside remains unchanged, significant long-term capital gains are unlikely. Other scenarios, from small long-term gains to sizable losses, are possible, depending on the magnitude and frequency of bull and bear markets. IWMI's share price is up 0.2% since inception, compared to 29.0% in gains for VTWO.

Similar performance moving forward seems like a reasonable assumption, although obviously equity returns could vary, as could the fund's covered call strategy. As mentioned previously, significant gains seem out of the question regardless.

IWMI's covered calls generate a significant amount of option premiums, resulting in a massive 13.5% distribution yield. It is significantly higher than that of its benchmark, and higher than most other covered call ETFs too.

IWMI's strong distribution yield is a significant, straightforward benefit for shareholders, and the core of IWMI's investment thesis.

IWMI's covered call strategy reduces upside potential, but results in a massive distribution yield. On a total return basis, the net effect should be positive when markets are flat or trending downwards, as the fund's massive distributions should outweigh any capital gains here. On the flipside, the net effect should be negative during more aggressive bull markets, as capital gains can easily outweigh a 13.5% distribution yield, high as that may be.

Long-term, slight underperformance seems likelier than not, as bull markets are more common than bear markets, considering the fund's elevated 0.68% expense ratio, and because covered calls are generally priced in such a way as to make this the likeliest scenario. Nevertheless, long-term performance should be strongly impacted by equity market returns, timing, and option premiums / prices.

Since inception, IWMI has slightly underperformed its benchmark, with a CAGR of 14.3% versus 15.7% for VTWO. Results are broadly consistent with the above, with the fund underperforming during a bull market. In my opinion, returns are quite positive and bullish for the fund, as underperformance is slight, equity market gains above-average. Seems like IWMI would have outperformed if the Russell had only seen 12%-13% in yearly gains, which are still quite high. Obviously there is no guarantee that IWMI's strategy will be as effective moving forward as it has in the past, but results so far are encouraging. Detailed data as per the fund's management:

Results for the fund, its benchmark, and the S&P 500 below. IWMI has slightly underperformed the S&P 500 since inception, but the comparison to its benchmark seems more relevant.

As an aside, IWMI had actually outperformed the S&P 500 when this article was originally published in the CEF/ETF Income Laboratory, you can see this above if you squint a bit. The actual change in relative performance was too small to matter, but I did have to remove one or two references to IWMI's 'outperformance'.

IWMI - Quick Peer Comparison

IWMI is one of many covered call ETFs in the market. Although each of these has specific characteristics, and compares to IWMI in specific ways, I think some generalities still apply.

IWMI's distribution yield is higher than that of its peers:

Returns have been higher too, owing to strong Russell 2000 results since inception. Some alpha seems possible, as the Neos S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI), from the same managers as IWMI, has outperformed most of its peers too.

IWMI's small-cap holdings are generally riskier than average, which should increase risk, volatility and, generally, drawdowns. With inception in early 2024, the fund has yet to experience a meaningful drawdown. VTWO is much older and did suffer significantly higher losses than the S&P 500 during early 2020, as expected. IWMI should have seen higher losses than its covered call peers too. Differences in covered call strategies could have impacted these results, although I don't think that the net effect would be all that different.

IWMI's equities trade at a discounted valuation than those of the S&P 500, and the same should be true of most of its covered call peers. In most cases, cheap valuations could lead to significant capital gains. In IWMI's case, the covered calls significantly limit upside potential, so any potential gains here are low regardless.

Conclusion

IWMI tracks the Russell 2000 and writes covered calls on a portion of its holdings. The fund's equity investments are riskier than average, but trade at a discounted valuation. IWMI's covered calls strategy significantly reduces upside potential, while boosting the fund's distribution yield to 13.5%. In my opinion, this is a reasonable tradeoff, and one which should be of interest to many income investors. IWMI is a strong investment opportunity, and a buy.

data.title: iRhythm: Structural Shift To Profitable Growth
data.description:
The recently released preliminary Q4 2025 results and 2026 guidance demonstrate a fundamental shift in the iRhythm (IRTC) business model based on increased operating leverage, increasing clinical indications, and the rapid expansion of its international footprint. My bullish thesis is founded upon the understanding that iRhythm is no longer merely competing for market share in the ambulatory cardiac monitoring space; it is actually expanding the total available market through the movement into primary care and adjacent comorbidities such as sleep apnea. I believe that combining the inevitable demographic trend with iRhythm's transition to double-digit adjusted EBITDA margin levels fundamentally alters the investment case from speculative growth to a quality compounder.

Recent Financials

For the full year 2025, iRhythm is forecasting revenues of over $740 million—above the high end of prior expectations; the result is due to the successful commercialization of the Zio platform at unprecedented levels of adoption, which translate directly to the bottom line. The company is providing a forecast of 2026 revenues of $870 million to $880 million; this represents a strong annual growth rate of about 17 percent to 18 percent, when compared to 2025. At a time when many in the sector are struggling to maintain low teens growth rates, this increased level of demand for the Zio platform shows the sustainable nature of the demand.

For the first time in the company’s history, iRhythm is forecasting being free cash flow positive for 2025. With respect to profitability, the 2026 outlook forecasts an adjusted EBITDA margin of 11.5% to 12.5%; for reference, the company was projecting a margin of 8.25% to 8.75% for 2025. The anticipated improvement in margins year-over-year is indicative of substantial operational leverage. The expected growth of revenue is outpacing growth in operating costs, allowing the company to create considerable cash without jeopardizing investment in R&D or commercial expansion.

This uptrend continues from the Q3 2025 financial report, where global net revenue was $192.9 million, with a gross profit margin of 71.1%. The stability of the gross profit margin, remaining relatively consistent with an average of 70%, as well as the impact of inflation on manufacturing and scale-up, speak to the effectiveness of the company’s automated manufacturing processes. As is necessary to grow the product pipeline, significant R&D expenses will continue to be incurred by the company. However, G&A overhead appears to be stabilizing relative to the company’s growing revenues. This financial structure, with high gross margins combined with disciplined management of operating expenses, supports my confidence in the company’s ability to meet the aggressive 2026 target margins.

Addressing The Cardiology Access Gap

A significant factor driving my thesis is the gap between what patients need in terms of specialist care and how many specialists are currently available. In the United States, according to the company, there are no cardiologists in 46.3% of counties in the country. The problem is much worse in rural areas. In rural America, 86.2% of counties have no access to specialist care. It has become normal for patients to be waiting 26.6 days for a non-urgent cardiology appointment. That number is increasing. The access gap creates a strong competitive advantage in both economics and medicine for iRhythm.

As a result of the lack of specialist cardiologists, primary care providers are increasingly responsible for making the initial diagnosis of cardiac conditions. Primary care doctors simply can't afford to wait 30 days to send a patient with palpitations to see a specialist. iRhythm's strategy to increase its penetration in the primary care provider market is necessary to help the overall healthcare system. iRhythm's Zio provides a rule-in/rule-out tool to streamline clinician workflow by allowing primary care physicians to either manage lower-risk patients directly or triage higher-risk patients more effectively.

iRhythm is pursuing this primary care market using a two-pronged approach: land-and-expand within integrated delivery networks and integrating with large national accounts. To reduce the friction associated with ordering and reporting, iRhythm has embedded its Zio platform into the electronic health record systems of large IDN networks. Of the 52% of registrations coming from accounts that are integrated with electronic health records, the stickiness of those registrations is critical to maintaining the long-term retention of those patients. As the population continues to age and the prevalence of heart rhythm disorders in the Medicare population continues to increase, the reliance on primary care for initial diagnoses will only increase, providing a tailwind to iRhythm's volume growth regardless of the general economic cycle.

Expanding into High-Value Adjacencies

In addition to serving the primary arrhythmia diagnosis market, iRhythm has many additional patient populations with high co-morbidities for atrial fibrillation where it is now actively opening opportunities. One of the most promising of these is Obstructive Sleep Apnea. According to the company, there are an estimated 40.1 million people in the United States who have sleep disorders; however, there is a large portion of this population that remains undiagnosed, primarily due to the difficulties associated with the traditional diagnostic process. Additionally, there is a significant clinical correlation between OSA and AF, as up to 80% of AF patients also experience sleep apnea.

The company stated that roughly 20% of the current healthcare providers utilizing iRhythm's products, are also prescribing sleep tests. With this overlap existing already in the physician community, iRhythm has a very low-friction cross-sell opportunity. By expanding their digital platform and independent diagnostic testing facility capabilities, iRhythm can extend its cardiac monitoring services to include home sleep testing. In doing so, iRhythm will transition itself from being a singular point-of-service for diagnosing arrhythmias into a larger platform for managing chronic diseases.

Additionally, the data presented on population health management in patients with Type 2 Diabetes, COPD, and Renal Disease was compelling. The data showed that patients with arrhythmias within these chronic disease populations were hospitalized over two times more frequently than those without arrhythmias and had significantly greater total costs ($17,200 vs. $1,700, respectively) than patients without arrhythmias. For organizations operating under a Value-Based Care model, this is a strong economic argument for early identification of arrhythmias through proactive use of Zio by payers and providers, thereby reducing the number of unnecessary hospitalizations.

International Markets as a Future Growth Engine

iRhythm is focusing on high-value markets, including the UK, Japan, and select European countries, including Switzerland, Spain, Austria, and the Netherlands. The total addressable market in these prioritized international regions is approximately 3.2 million tests annually, which is similar to the size of the current U.S. core market.

The UK serves as the model for this type of expansion, where iRhythm has identified an opportunity for 700,000 tests annually. The company has moved beyond early commercialization and is now securing ongoing secondary care contracts in addition to expanding into primary care; a strategy mirrored in its U.S. operations. The most interesting international opportunity is Japan, where, according to the company, there are 1.6 million annual ambulatory cardiac monitoring tests, making it the second-largest global market for this technology. iRhythm is currently in the early commercialization phase there; it plans on generating local evidence by conducting head-to-head studies against Japanese devices to increase physician awareness.

Success in these markets will require the company to navigate complex reimbursement landscapes, but the payoff could be substantial. International revenue will reduce the risk profile of the company by proving the transferability of the business model. As these markets mature, they will likely follow the U.S. trajectory of margin expansion, contributing to the long-term free cash flow profile of the company.

Valuation Context

The revenue estimate for 2026 of $870 million to $880 million and the midpoint of the 12% Adjusted EBITDA margin are solid, but iRhythm is trading at multiples significantly higher than those of the sector medians of medical technology and diagnostic companies. From a value investor’s perspective, the company may seem overvalued, but I would argue that one needs to take a growth perspective to properly understand the current context.

iRhythm is compounding revenue at nearly 20% per annum at scale; simultaneously expanding margins significantly per year. This financial profile is rare in the MedTech sector. Therefore, the valuation premium paid for iRhythm is justified based on the sustainability of the growth—anchored in the 40% penetration rate of the core market and the enormous greenfield opportunities in primary care and international markets—and the lack of other high-growth and free cash flow-positive assets in the sector.

Additionally, with adjusted EBITDA projected to increase from the single-digit percentages in 2025 to greater than 12% in 2026, I think the currently unfavorable forward-looking multiples will compress dramatically. As long as iRhythm executes on its margin expansion plan, the company can grow into a more favorable valuation through earnings power as opposed to multiple expansion.

Risks and Counterarguments

One of the more immediate concerns I see is the FDA approval timeline for the Zio MCT. Although iRhythm filed the 510(k) in Q3 2025, the product has not received clearance. Therefore, if the FDA has unforeseen issues with either the submission process or requests for additional data, the commercialization date for the product could be delayed significantly, thereby causing the expected revenue to be reduced.

Another area of risk is competition. iRhythm estimates that there were 1.9 million short-duration monitors (legacy monitors) used in the US today, and iRhythm views this as a conversion opportunity with $500M in potential revenue. However, the legacy monitors have several characteristics that make them attractive alternatives to the premium patch based monitor offered by iRhythm. Specifically, they are much less expensive, which is becoming increasingly important in today’s environment of cost-cutting measures in the healthcare industry. This could prevent some hospitals from converting to a premium patch-based solution, especially for those patients who do not require extensive testing.

Additionally, the expansion into primary care represents another significant challenge with respect to execution risk. The commercial approach required to sell to primary care physicians is fundamentally different from the commercial approach to sell to cardiologists. Specifically, the sales cycle is longer, the initial revenue per account is much lower, and the customer base is much more fragmented than the customer base of cardiologists. If iRhythm cannot effectively expand its sales force or digital marketing capabilities to efficiently address the needs of primary care physicians, then the costs associated with acquiring customers may increase, which will create pressure on the margin structure that is critical to my thesis.

Future Outlook

Despite these risks, I believe iRhythm is well positioned to change the paradigm of how we diagnose cardiac disease. Their proprietary biosensor technology, advanced algorithms to analyze the data generated, and efficient and seamless clinical workflow model, have combined to create a barrier to entry that will be quite daunting for potential competitors. The execution demonstrated in iRhythm's 2025 fiscal year-end results and the clarity of the company's 2026 strategic plan provide a solid basis for an attractive entry point. The shift to generating positive free cash flow transforms the nature of the investment in iRhythm, and for investors who desire exposure to a quality digital health company that has transitioned to the profit stage of its growth trajectory, I think iRhythm is definitely interesting.

data.title: Silver And Gold: The Rise And Fall Set Up An Option Play
data.description:
Silver and gold volatility reached extreme levels

My goals for this article are twofold. I want to A) explain why investors should be prepared for persisting high volatilities for both silver and gold, and B) explain why I see a favorable setup to sell options under current market conditions. In recent trading data, I have witnessed some of the most extreme volatilities in silver and gold prices in my experience (and I have been investing in both metals for more than a decade by now). As an example, the next chart illustrates the percentage decrease from recent price highs for the iShares Silver Trust (SLV) and SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) over the past month. You can see how both metals, after staging some of the most spectacular bull runs in the past year, suffered their sharp downturns. Specifically, SLV plummeted almost 33% off its high, and GLD dropped more than 12% off its high.

In tandem with the price volatility, the implied volatility (IV) for both metals has already reached extreme levels in the option market. For instance, the screenshot below was taken from MarketChameleon and provides the IV of SLV. As seen, SLV's IV is currently 110, approximately tripling its 52-week historical average of 34 and only about 5 points shy of its 52-week peak of 115. In terms of percentile, SLV's current IV puts it in the 99th percentile rank. In other words, the current level of IV was only observed 1% of the time in the past year.

Against this background, I will next explain that such heightened volatilities could persist for both metals.

Be prepared for persisting high volatilities

Looking ahead, I anticipate such elevated volatility to persist for both gold and silver due to several reasons. My anticipation is largely based on considerations that fall into three buckets: lack of technical guidance, ongoing geopolitical risks, and the conflicting forces for and against a potential short squeeze.

Let me start with the first one. Despite the sharp corrections, the prices of both metals are still well above their multi-year (or even multi-decade) resistance levels. As such, I expect both swing traders and long-term investors to keep dealing with the lack of technical guidance to anchor their future trades and target prices.

As for the geopolitical risks, a key catalyst behind the recent rise and fall of precious metal prices is the uncertainties surrounding the Federal Reserve's next leadership, which in turn leads to the uncertainties regarding the Fed's future pivots. Even though President Trump has just confirmed his nomination of Kevin Warsh as the next Fed chair (which the market interpreted as a net negative for gold and silver), I won't automatically assume that this nomination has completely ended the uncertainties on this front. The nomination still needs to be confirmed, and other ramifications could develop in the meantime.

Finally, there is also a potential for a short squeeze, especially for silver, given the violent price swings and also the inventory decrease lately. As a reflection of this potential, the next chart shows COMEX's silver inventory in the past 1 year. As seen, since Sept 2025, the inventory has experienced a sharp decline from around 550 million oz to the current level of 408 million only. The ongoing price correction certainly helps to counterbalance the odds of a short squeeze. In the meantime, other counterforces have also been set in motion, with regulatory intervention among the top forces on my list (see a recent announcement made by the Shanghai Futures Exchange below). I don't expect such a confluence of conflicting forces to find their equilibrium point smoothly and/or quickly and expect continued volatility in the process.

CaiXing Global News: The Shanghai Futures Exchange on Monday tightened trading rules for key metals for the second time in a week, seeking to tamp down excessive market volatility after a dramatic surge in silver and gold prices. Starting after Wednesday's close, the exchange will expand daily price limits for copper and aluminum futures to 9% and raise margin requirements for speculative positions to 11%. Similar adjustments will be applied to international copper contracts. In addition, daily opening position limits for non-futures-company members and clients trading silver and tin will be reduced starting from Monday night's trading session.

Gold and silver: options

Given such extreme volatility, I see a good setup for investors to sell options. For investors unfamiliar with options, when IV rises, the option sellers receive a higher premium income from the option buyers. Probably it is easier to explain the dynamics with a specific example. The table below shows the put option chain for the SLV ETF with an expiry of Feb 27, 2026, which is less than 1 month away from today. For an NTM (near-the-money) option with a strike price of $75, the option last traded at about $10.93 per share. In other words, the seller of this option gets paid almost 15% of the share price upfront in exchange for the obligation to buy SLV shares by or on Feb 27 at a price of $75. If the option is not executed, the seller thus effectively earned a 15% yield in less than 1 month (assuming the option is covered by cash). In the case the option is assigned, the seller would have effectively bought SLV shares at about $64 per share only, a quite attractive entry point for long-term holding if you share my following outlook.

But as a strong word of caution, the risk calculus for trading options can be very different from direct share ownership. I don't want my above discussion to mislead you to believe that there are no risks for option sellers either when the option expires or gets assigned. When viewed from the perspective of opportunity cost, the risks for option sellers can be unlimited.

Other risks and final thoughts

My above discussion on the put option is equally applicable to investors interested in call options. I used the example of a put option largely due to my personal view on the long-term prospects of silver. As you could have already guessed, my analysis with the option play is targeting the immediate term (1 or a few months). For the longer term, I have a quite positive view toward silver and gold. I see our transition towards green energies and a digital future as an unstoppable trend and expect this trend to keep expanding the industrial demand for silver. For gold, I expect continued demand from central banks as they diversify their currency reserves. These reasons are further detailed in my other articles (for example, a recent one explores the similarities and differences between today's situation and the so-called Silver Thursday in 1980). Given such a long-term view, I prefer the use of a put option as a potential way to accumulate more silver shares.

Before closing, let's also take a quick look at GLD's IV and options. The screenshot below is also taken from MarketChameleon. It shows the current IV of GLD, and the picture is just as extreme as that for SLV. As seen, GLD's IV is currently 42.5, more than double its 52-week average of 18.5. In terms of percentile, GLD's current IV is also in the 99th percentile rank, just like SLV. As such, most of my comments regarding SLV's options in the foregoing section are perfectly extendable to GLD options in my mind. Again, I personally would prefer the use of put options to accumulate more shares at a lower cost basis for long-term holding.

To conclude, the thesis of this article is mainly twofold. Firstly, I wanted to caution investors about the factors that could keep volatilities heightened for silver and gold. Secondly and more importantly, I wanted to explain why I see a favorable setup for option sellers under this outlook. Investors who want to wind down their precious metal exposure should take a look at call options, and investors who want to maintain (or increase exposure like me) should consider put options.
